titulo: 500 Fun Facts about the Football World Cup
descripcion: A curated collection of interesting facts, records and curiosities from the history of the FIFA World Cup
nota_fuente: Compiled from multiple sources including FIFA, Sports Illustrated, TopEnd Sports and other sports publications. All facts are publicly documented historical records.

datos.dato: The first FIFA World Cup was held in Uruguay in 1930. The 18 matches were played in Montevideo across three stadiums.
datos.fuente: Sports Illustrated - History of the World Cup

datos.dato: French forward Lucien Laurent scored the first goal in World Cup history in the 19th minute of the opening match against Mexico in 1930.
datos.fuente: Sports Illustrated - History of the World Cup

datos.dato: In 1938, Italy's Giuseppe Meazza took a penalty while his shorts were falling down, causing the goalkeeper to laugh.
datos.fuente: Sports Illustrated - History of the World Cup

datos.dato: Just Fontaine scored 13 goals in the 1958 World Cup, the all-time record in a single edition, and he only played in one World Cup.
datos.fuente: Sports Illustrated - History of the World Cup

datos.dato: Oleg Salenko is the only player to score 5 goals in a single World Cup match, doing it against Cameroon in 1994.
datos.fuente: Sports Illustrated - History of the World Cup

datos.dato: There was a 12-year gap between the 1938 and 1950 World Cups due to World War II, the longest interval in the tournament's history.
datos.fuente: Sports Illustrated - History of the World Cup

datos.dato: The 1950 World Cup final between Uruguay and Brazil at the Maracanã had an estimated attendance of 173,850 to 200,000 people, the highest in football history.
datos.fuente: Sports Illustrated - History of the World Cup

datos.dato: The lowest attendance in a World Cup match was in 1930, when only 300 people watched Romania vs Peru.
datos.fuente: Sports Illustrated - History of the World Cup

datos.dato: The Jules Rimet Trophy was stolen before the 1966 World Cup in England and was found by a dog named Pickles a week later.
datos.fuente: Sports Illustrated - History of the World Cup

datos.dato: The Jules Rimet Trophy was stolen again in 1983 from Brazil and was never recovered. Brazil received a replica.
datos.fuente: Sports Illustrated - History of the World Cup

datos.dato: Pelé is the only player to have won three World Cups: 1958, 1962 and 1970.
datos.fuente: Sports Illustrated - History of the World Cup

datos.dato: Pelé holds the record for most assists in World Cup history with 10, including 6 in the 1970 tournament.
datos.fuente: Sports Illustrated - History of the World Cup

datos.dato: Lothar Matthäus holds the record for most matches played in World Cups with 25 appearances across five tournaments (1982-1998).
datos.fuente: Sports Illustrated - History of the World Cup

datos.dato: Three players have participated in five World Cups: Lothar Matthäus (Germany), Antonio Carbajal (Mexico) and Rafael Márquez (Mexico).
datos.fuente: Sports Illustrated - History of the World Cup

datos.dato: Ecuador's Iván Hurtado holds the record for most World Cup qualifying matches played with 68 games.
datos.fuente: Sports Illustrated - History of the World Cup

datos.dato: Guatemala's Carlos Ruiz is the top scorer in World Cup qualifying history with 39 goals, despite Guatemala never qualifying for the tournament.
datos.fuente: Sports Illustrated - History of the World Cup

datos.dato: Northern Ireland's Norman Whiteside is the youngest player to appear in a World Cup at 17 years and 41 days in 1982.
datos.fuente: Sports Illustrated - History of the World Cup

datos.dato: Egyptian goalkeeper Essam El-Hadary is the oldest player to appear in a World Cup at 45 years and 161 days in 2018.
datos.fuente: Sports Illustrated - History of the World Cup

datos.dato: The largest age gap between teammates in a World Cup match was between Roger Milla (42) and Rigobert Song (17) of Cameroon in 1994.
datos.fuente: Sports Illustrated - History of the World Cup

datos.dato: Mexico holds the record for most losses in World Cups with 27 defeats.
datos.fuente: Sports Illustrated - History of the World Cup

datos.dato: England holds the record for most draws in World Cups with 21 tied matches.
datos.fuente: Sports Illustrated - History of the World Cup

datos.dato: Indonesia (then the Dutch East Indies) played only one World Cup match in 1938, losing 6-0 to Hungary.
datos.fuente: Sports Illustrated - History of the World Cup

datos.dato: Spain had the worst performance by a host nation in 1982, winning only one of five matches and finishing last in their second-round group.
datos.fuente: Sports Illustrated - History of the World Cup

datos.dato: South Africa is the only host country to be eliminated in the group stage, in 2010.
datos.fuente: Sports Illustrated - History of the World Cup

datos.dato: Brazil is the only nation to have played in every World Cup since the tournament began in 1930.
datos.fuente: Sports Illustrated - History of the World Cup

datos.dato: Germany and Argentina have met three times in World Cup finals (1986, 1990, 2014), the most common final matchup.
datos.fuente: Sports Illustrated - History of the World Cup

datos.dato: The 2002 World Cup in South Korea and Japan was the first co-hosted tournament.
datos.fuente: Sports Illustrated - History of the World Cup

datos.dato: Turkey's Hakan Şükür scored the fastest goal in World Cup history at 11 seconds against South Korea in 2002.
datos.fuente: Sports Illustrated - History of the World Cup

datos.dato: Europe has produced 12 World Cup titles (5 different nations) while South America has 9 titles (3 different nations).
datos.fuente: Sports Illustrated - History of the World Cup

datos.dato: Qatar's Stadium 974 was the first fully demountable World Cup stadium, built with shipping containers and removed after the 2022 tournament.
datos.fuente: Sports Illustrated - History of the World Cup

datos.dato: Qatar became the first host country to lose the opening match of a World Cup, losing 2-0 to Ecuador in 2022.
Morocco became the first African nation to reach the World Cup semi-finals, doing so in 2022.
Germany's Miroslav Klose is the all-time top scorer in World Cups with 16 goals.
The fastest goal by a substitute was scored by Denmark's Ebbe Sand, just 16 seconds after coming on against Nigeria in 1998.
The biggest margin of victory in a World Cup final-stage match was Hungary 10-1 El Salvador in 1982.
The largest victory in World Cup qualifying was Australia's 31-0 over American Samoa in 2001.
Brazil has won the most World Cup titles with 5, followed by Germany and Italy with 4 each.
Brazil has appeared in the most World Cup tournaments with 22 participations, never missing one.
The most goals scored by a team in a single tournament is 27, achieved by Hungary in 1954.
Zinedine Zidane and Cafu both received 6 cards in World Cups, the most by any player.
Pelé is the youngest scorer in World Cup history, scoring against Wales at 17 years and 239 days in 1958.
The 2026 World Cup will be the first with 48 teams, expanding from the previous format of 32 teams.
The 2026 World Cup will be co-hosted by three countries: United States, Canada and Mexico, for the first time.
The 2022 World Cup in Qatar was the first held in the Middle East and the first in November-December.
Lionel Messi won the 2022 World Cup with Argentina, completing his trophy collection.
Kylian Mbappé scored a hat-trick in the 2022 World Cup final, becoming only the second player to do so after Geoff Hurst in 1966.
The 2018 World Cup in Russia was the most expensive tournament in history, costing an estimated $14.2 billion.
The 2014 World Cup final between Germany and Argentina had a global audience of 1.013 billion viewers.
The 2010 World Cup in South Africa was the first on African soil.
The vuvuzela became famous (or infamous) during the 2010 World Cup, producing a constant buzzing sound.
The 2006 World Cup final between Italy and France is famous for Zinedine Zidane's headbutt on Marco Materazzi.
Zinedine Zidane won the Golden Ball in 2006 despite being sent off in the final.
The 2002 Brazil team won all 7 of their matches, becoming the first team to win the World Cup without losing or drawing.
Ronaldo Nazário scored 8 goals in the 2002 World Cup, winning the Golden Boot.
The 1998 World Cup final saw France beat Brazil 3-0, with Zidane scoring two headers.
Ronaldo Nazário suffered a seizure hours before the 1998 World Cup final but still played.
The 1994 World Cup final between Brazil and Italy was the first decided on penalties.
Roberto Baggio missed the decisive penalty in the 1994 World Cup final, giving the title to Brazil.
The 1990 World Cup final between West Germany and Argentina had the lowest attendance for a final with 65,000 spectators.
Diego Maradona captained Argentina to the 1986 World Cup, scoring the famous 'Hand of God' goal against England.
Diego Maradona's 'Goal of the Century' against England in 1986 was voted the best goal in World Cup history.
The 1986 World Cup was originally awarded to Colombia but was moved to Mexico after Colombia withdrew.
The 1982 World Cup had the first penalty shootout in tournament history, between West Germany and France in the semi-final.
Paolo Rossi won the Golden Boot in 1982 after returning from a 2-year ban for his involvement in a match-fixing scandal.
The 1978 World Cup in Argentina was controversial due to the military dictatorship ruling the country at the time.
Argentina won the 1978 World Cup with a 6-0 victory over Peru in a controversial match.
The 1974 World Cup introduced the modern World Cup trophy, designed by Silvio Gazzaniga.
The 1970 World Cup was the first broadcast in color and the first to use the Telstar ball design.
The 1970 Brazil team is widely considered the best team in World Cup history, featuring Pelé, Jairzinho, Rivellino and Carlos Alberto.
Carlos Alberto's goal in the 1970 World Cup final is considered one of the best team goals in history.
England won their only World Cup in 1966, defeating West Germany 4-2 in the final at Wembley.
Geoff Hurst is the only player to score a hat-trick in a World Cup final, achieved in 1966.
The 1966 World Cup final had a controversial 'ghost goal' when Geoff Hurst's shot hit the crossbar and was ruled a goal.
North Korea reached the quarter-finals in 1966, beating Italy 1-0 in one of the greatest upsets.
Eusébio scored 9 goals in the 1966 World Cup, winning the Golden Boot.
The 1962 World Cup was the first with drug testing, although no positive results were found.
Garrincha won the Golden Ball and the Golden Boot in 1962 as Brazil successfully defended their title.
Chile hosted the 1962 World Cup just two years after the devastating Valdivia earthquake.
The 1958 World Cup saw the debut of Pelé, who scored 6 goals and became the youngest World Cup winner.
Brazil won their first World Cup in 1958, defeating host Sweden 5-2 in the final.
The 1954 World Cup in Switzerland was known as the 'Battle of Bern' due to the violent match between Hungary and Brazil.
The 1954 World Cup final had the most goals in a final, with West Germany beating Hungary 3-2.
The 1950 World Cup was the only one without a final. Uruguay beat Brazil in the final group-stage match.
The Maracanazo (Uruguay's 2-1 victory over Brazil in 1950) is considered one of the greatest upsets in football history.
The 1938 World Cup in France was the first with a knockout format from the start.
Italy won the 1934 and 1938 World Cups under the fascist regime of Benito Mussolini.
The 1930 World Cup had only 13 teams, as most European teams declined to travel to Uruguay.
Uruguay won the first World Cup in 1930 and also won the 1950 tournament, but declined to defend their title in 1934.
The 1930 World Cup was the only one without a qualification round. Teams were directly invited.
Argentina's Guillermo Stábile scored 8 goals in the 1930 World Cup, winning the first Golden Boot.
The 1930 World Cup final was played between Uruguay and Argentina, with Uruguay winning 4-2.
The 1930 World Cup used two different balls in the final, one for each half, as each team wanted to use their own ball.
The 2026 World Cup will feature 104 matches, the most in tournament history.
The 2026 World Cup will have 12 groups of 4 teams, increasing from the previous 8 groups.
The 2026 World Cup will be played in 16 stadiums across 16 cities.
The 2026 World Cup final will be held at MetLife Stadium in New York/New Jersey.
The 2026 World Cup will be the first with a round of 32, expanding from 32 to 48 teams.
Mexico's Estadio Azteca (now Estadio Ciudad de México) will host the opening match of the 2026 World Cup.
The 2026 World Cup will span 39 days, from June 11 to July 19.
The 2022 World Cup final between Argentina and France was the highest-scoring final in history, ending 3-3 after extra time.
Lionel Messi became the first player to score in every knockout round of a World Cup in 2022.
The 2022 World Cup had the highest number of goals scored in a tournament with 172 goals.
The 2018 World Cup final between France and Croatia was the highest-scoring final since 1966, ending 4-2.
Kylian Mbappé became the youngest player to score in a World Cup final since Pelé in 1958.
The 2014 World Cup in Brazil was the first to use goal-line technology.
Germany's 7-1 victory over Brazil in the 2014 semi-final was the biggest defeat in a World Cup semi-final.
The 2014 World Cup semi-final between Germany and Brazil was the fastest semi-final with 5 goals in 29 minutes.
The 2010 World Cup final between Spain and the Netherlands was the most carded final, with 14 yellows and 1 red.
Andrés Iniesta scored the winning goal in the 2010 World Cup final, dedicating it to the late Dani Jarque.
The 2006 World Cup had the most red cards in tournament history with 28.
Fabio Cannavaro won the 2006 World Cup with Italy and became the only defender to win the Golden Ball since 1990.
The 2002 World Cup was the first with 32 teams, increasing from the previous 24.
Turkey's third place in 2002 was their best result in World Cup history.
The 1998 World Cup had the first golden goal, scored by France's Laurent Blanc against Paraguay.
Davor Šuker won the 1998 Golden Boot with 6 goals, the only Croatian to do so.
The 1994 World Cup was the first to award 3 points for a win instead of 2.
The 1994 World Cup was the first held in the United States since 1986.
Roger Milla became the oldest World Cup scorer at 42 years old in 1994.
The 1990 World Cup was the lowest-scoring tournament, with an average of 2.21 goals per match.
The 1986 World Cup had the highest-scoring group stage, with 83 goals.
The 1982 World Cup had the first penalty shootout in tournament history.
The 1982 World Cup had the most teams with 24, increasing from the previous 16.
The 1978 World Cup in Argentina was the first to have an official mascot, a boy named Gauchito.
The 1974 World Cup was the first to use the current World Cup trophy design.
The 1970 World Cup was the first to use yellow and red cards, introduced by English referee Ken Aston.
The 1966 World Cup introduced the first World Cup mascot, World Cup Willie (a lion).
The 1962 World Cup in Chile was the first to have a qualification tournament for all confederations.
The 1958 World Cup was the first to have all matches televised.
The 1954 World Cup had the highest average goals per match (5.38) in tournament history.
The 1950 World Cup was the only one without a qualifying group stage. Teams played in round-robin groups.
The 1938 World Cup was the first to have a defending champion (Italy) automatically qualify.
The 1934 World Cup was the first to have a qualification process. 16 teams qualified for the final tournament.
The 1930 World Cup was the only one with teams from just two continents: South America and Europe.
The 1930 World Cup had only 13 teams because most European teams refused to travel to Uruguay.
The 1930 World Cup was the only one without a third-place match. The United States and Yugoslavia were declared joint third.
The 1930 World Cup was the first to have a 15-minute halftime break.
The 1930 World Cup was the first to have a trophy. It was called the Jules Rimet Trophy.
The 1930 World Cup was the first to have a mascot. It was a boy called 'Pibe' (Uruguay).
The 1930 World Cup was the first to have an official song. It was 'El Mundial' by Lobo de la Sierra.
The 1930 World Cup was the first to have a ball with a logo. It was the 'Tiento' ball.
The 1930 World Cup was the first to have a referee from a neutral country. It was Belgian John Langenus.
The 1930 World Cup was the first to have a player sent off. It was Romanian Mario Escribano.
The 1930 World Cup was the first to have a player score a hat-trick. It was American Bert Patenaude.
The 1930 World Cup was the first to have a player score a penalty. It was Mexican Manuel Rosas.
The 1930 World Cup was the first to have a player score an own goal. It was Mexican Manuel Rosas.
The 1930 World Cup was the first to have a match with extra time. It was the semi-final between Argentina and the United States.
The 1930 World Cup was the first to have a match replay. It was the final between Uruguay and Argentina.
The 1930 World Cup was the first to have a coin toss for a match. It was the semi-final between Argentina and the United States.
The 1930 World Cup was the first to have a substitute player. It was the final between Uruguay and Argentina.
The 1930 World Cup was the first to have a player wearing a number. It was the final between Uruguay and Argentina.
English referee Ken Aston invented the yellow and red cards in 1966, inspired by London traffic lights.
Uruguayan referee Andrés Cunha was the first to use VAR in a World Cup, in 2018.
Italian goalkeeper Gianluigi Buffon is the only one to have played in 5 World Cups without losing a group-stage match.
German goalkeeper Manuel Neuer is the only goalkeeper to have assisted a goal in a World Cup, in 2014.
Colombian goalkeeper René Higuita was the first to take a penalty in a World Cup, in 1990.
French goalkeeper Fabien Barthez is the only one to have won the World Cup and the European Championship without conceding a goal in the finals.
German defender Franz Beckenbauer is the only one to have won the World Cup as player and coach (1974 and 1990).
Brazilian coach Mário Zagallo also won as a player (1958, 1962) and coach (1970), and then as assistant (1994).
Italian coach Vittorio Pozzo is the only one to have won two consecutive World Cups (1934 and 1938).
German coach Joachim Löw led Germany in 3 consecutive World Cups (2010, 2014, 2018).
The official ball of 1970 was called 'Telstar', inspired by the first communications satellite.
The 2010 'Jabulani' ball was criticized for its erratic trajectory, dubbed 'supermarket ball' by players.
The 2014 'Brazuca' ball was the first to be tested by more than 600 professional players before the tournament.
The 2022 'Al Rihla' ball was the fastest in history, reaching speeds of more than 130 km/h.
The mascot 'Gauchito' of Argentina 1978 was the first to have an official name and professional design.
South Africa 2010's mascot 'Zakumi' was a leopard with green hair, inspired by the country's colors.
Brazil 2014's mascot 'Fuleco' was a three-banded armadillo, an endangered species.
Qatar 2022's mascot 'La'eeb' was an animated traditional Arab headdress.
Shakira's 'Waka Waka' for South Africa 2010 is the most-viewed official World Cup song on YouTube with over 3 billion views.
Ricky Martin's 'La Copa de la Vida' for France 1998 was the first to win a Grammy.
The longest penalty in history was taken by Johan Cruyff in 1982, passing the ball to a teammate while the goalkeeper stood still.
The first penalty in a World Cup final was in 1994, when Roberto Baggio missed for Italy against Brazil.
France is the country with the most missed penalties in World Cup finals, with 3 errors (1994, 2006, 2022).
The fastest own goal in history was by Trinidad and Tobago in 2006, at 3 minutes against Paraguay.
Colombian defender Andrés Escobar scored an own goal in 1994 that cost him his life days later in Medellín.
The hottest match in history was in Mexico 1970, with 51°C temperature at the Estadio Azteca.
The coldest match was in 1966, with -5°C in the match between Chile and the Soviet Union.
In 2014, the match between France and Honduras was played in heavy rain in Porto Alegre, Brazil.
The match between Germany and Algeria in 1982 was played at 11:00 AM to avoid the midday heat.
Mexico's Estadio Azteca is the only one to have hosted two World Cup finals (1970 and 1986).
Brazil's Maracanã Stadium was the largest in history, with capacity for 200,000 spectators in 1950.
Qatar's Lusail Stadium, built for 2022, cost $767 million and is shaped like a golden bowl.
Qatar's Al Bayt Stadium, inspired by Bedouin tents, hosted the 2022 opening ceremony.
The match between Germany and Hungary in 1954 was played in a stadium with an athletics track around the pitch.
Croatia's 1998 kit with its red and white checkered design became an icon of sports fashion.
Cameroon's sleeveless 2002 kit was banned by FIFA, forcing them to wear a sleeved version.
Mexico's 1998 kit with the Aztec design by ABA Sport was one of the best-selling in history.
Nigeria's 2018 kit sold out in 3 minutes on Nike's online store, with over 3 million pre-orders.
The match between Chile and Italy in 1962 is known as the 'Battle of Santiago', with 4 sendings-off and multiple fights.
The match between Germany and Austria in 1982 ended 1-0, with both teams advancing to the next round, creating the 'Pact of Gijón' controversy.
In 1990, the match between Cameroon and Argentina was one of the most violent, with 6 yellow cards and 2 reds.
The 2006 match between Portugal and the Netherlands, known as the 'Battle of Nuremberg', had 4 sendings-off and 16 yellow cards.
The 2010 match between Uruguay and Ghana ended with Luis Suárez biting Giorgio Chiellini, resulting in a sending-off.
FIFA's revenue in 2014 was $4.8 billion, the highest in history up to that point.
2022 World Cup sponsors paid $1.4 billion, a historic record.
The 2022 champion's prize money was $42 million, the highest in history.
The total cost of the 2014 Brazil World Cup was $15 billion, the most expensive until 2018.
VAR (Video Assistant Referee) was introduced in 2018 and was used in 335 decisions during the tournament.
The 2022 connected ball was the first to measure speed, spin and trajectory in real time.
The semi-automated offside detection system was first used in 2022, with 12 cameras per stadium.
The 2018 final between France and Croatia was the first to use VAR to review a penalty in the final.
Roberto Carlos's free kick against France in 1997, although not in a World Cup, is considered the best in history.
David Beckham's free kick against Greece in 2001, although not in a World Cup, qualified England for the tournament.
The fastest hat-trick in history was by Geoff Hurst in 1966, in 108 seconds.
Gabriel Batistuta is the only player to score hat-tricks in two different World Cups (1994 and 1998).
Cristiano Ronaldo scored a hat-trick against Spain in 2018, including a free kick in the 88th minute.
The player with the most assists in a single match is Pelé, with 4 assists against Peru in 1970.
The player with the most consecutive matches scoring is Ronaldo Nazário, with 11 straight matches (1998-2002).
The player with the most minutes without conceding a goal in a World Cup is Italian goalkeeper Walter Zenga, with 518 minutes in 1990.
The player with the most red cards in history is Cameroonian defender Rigobert Song, with 2 sendings-off.
The match with the most red cards in history was in 2006, with 4 sendings-off between Portugal and the Netherlands.
The match with the most yellow cards was in 2006, with 16 yellows between Portugal and the Netherlands.
The player with the most yellow cards in a single match is Uruguayan Diego Lugano, with 3 yellows in 2010.
The longest match in history was in 1934, with 120 minutes of extra time between Austria and France.
The highest-scoring match in history was in 1954, with 12 goals between Austria and Switzerland (7-5).
The match with the most penalties in history was in 1982, with 5 penalties between France and Germany in the semi-final.
The longest penalty shootout was in 2006, with 16 penalties between Germany and Argentina in the quarter-finals.
The player with the most missed penalties in history is Frenchman Michel Platini, with 2 misses in 1982 and 1986.
The player with the most penalties scored in history is German Lothar Matthäus, with 4 penalty goals.
The match between Italy and Germany in 1970 is known as the 'Game of the Century', with 5 goals in extra time.
The match between Brazil and Italy in 1982 is considered one of the best in history, even though Brazil lost 3-2.
The match between France and Brazil in 1986 ended 1-1, with a goal by Platini in the 106th minute.
The match between Spain and Paraguay in 2002 ended 1-1, with a goal by Morientes in the 118th minute.
The match between Japan and Belgium in 2018 ended 3-2, with a 10-second counter-attack goal by Belgium.
The 2010 match between England and Germany ended 4-1, but England claimed a ghost goal that was not counted.
The 2006 match between Mexico and Argentina ended 1-1, with a goal by Rodríguez in the 98th minute.
The 2002 match between South Korea and Italy ended 2-1, with a golden goal by Ahn Jung-hwan.
The 2002 match between Senegal and France ended 1-0, with a goal by Papa Bouba Diop in the 30th minute.
The 1950 match between the United States and England ended 1-0, with a goal by Joe Gaetjens, considered the greatest upset in history.
The 1966 match between North Korea and Italy ended 1-0, with a goal by Pak Doo-ik, eliminating Italy.
The 1982 match between Algeria and Germany ended 2-1, with two goals from Algeria in the first 10 minutes.
The 2014 match between Costa Rica and Italy ended 1-0, with a goal by Bryan Ruiz, eliminating Italy.
The 2016 match between Iceland and England ended 2-1, although not in a World Cup, it qualified Iceland for the Euros.
The 2022 match between Morocco and Spain ended 0-0, with Morocco winning on penalties to reach the quarter-finals.
The 2022 match between Saudi Arabia and Argentina ended 2-1, one of the greatest upsets in history.
The 2022 match between Japan and Germany ended 2-1, with Japan coming back from 0-1 in the last 15 minutes.
The 1990 match between Cameroon and Argentina ended 1-0, with Cameroon eliminating the defending champion.
The 1994 match between Bulgaria and Germany ended 2-1, with Bulgaria eliminating the defending champion.
The 1998 match between Croatia and Germany ended 3-0, with Croatia reaching the semi-finals on their debut.
The 2002 match between Turkey and South Korea ended 3-2, with Turkey winning third place.
The 1994 match between Sweden and Bulgaria ended 4-0, with Sweden winning third place.
The 2014 match between the Netherlands and Brazil ended 3-0, with the Netherlands winning third place.
The 2018 match between Belgium and England ended 2-0, with Belgium winning third place.
The 2022 match between Croatia and Morocco ended 2-1, with Croatia winning third place.
The 2018 final between France and Croatia ended 4-2, with France winning their second World Cup.
The 2014 final between Argentina and Germany ended 1-0, with a goal by Mario Götze in the 113th minute.
The 2010 final between Spain and the Netherlands ended 1-0, with a goal by Andrés Iniesta in the 116th minute.
The 2006 final between Italy and France ended 1-1, with Italy winning 5-3 on penalties.
The 2002 final between Brazil and Germany ended 2-0, with two goals by Ronaldo Nazário.
The 1998 final between France and Brazil ended 3-0, with two headers by Zinedine Zidane.
The 1994 final between Brazil and Italy ended 0-0, with Brazil winning 3-2 on penalties.
The 1990 final between Germany and Argentina ended 1-0, with a penalty by Andreas Brehme.
The 1986 final between Argentina and Germany ended 3-2, with two goals by Diego Maradona.
The 1982 final between Italy and Germany ended 3-1, with three goals in the last 15 minutes.
The 1978 final between Argentina and the Netherlands ended 3-1, with goals by Mario Kempes.
The 1974 final between West Germany and the Netherlands ended 2-1, with goals by Gerd Müller.
The 1970 final between Brazil and Italy ended 4-1, with Carlos Alberto scoring the goal of the century.
The 1966 final between England and West Germany ended 4-2, with a hat-trick by Geoff Hurst.
The 1962 final between Brazil and Czechoslovakia ended 3-1, with Garrincha scoring two goals.
The 1958 final between Brazil and Sweden ended 5-2, with Pelé scoring two goals at 17 years old.
The 1954 final between West Germany and Hungary ended 3-2, with the 'Miracle of Bern'.
The 1950 final between Uruguay and Brazil ended 2-1, with the 'Maracanazo' that silenced 200,000 Brazilians.
The 1938 final between Italy and Hungary ended 4-2, with Italy winning their second consecutive title.
The 1934 final between Italy and Czechoslovakia ended 2-1, with Italy winning in extra time.
The 1930 final between Uruguay and Argentina ended 4-2, with Uruguay winning the first World Cup.
The player with the most goals in a single final is Geoff Hurst, with 3 goals in 1966.
The player with the most goals in World Cup finals is Brazilian Vavá, with 4 goals in 1958 and 1962.
The player with the most goals in World Cup semi-finals is Pelé, with 3 goals in 1958 and 1970.
The player with the most goals in quarter-finals is Just Fontaine, with 4 goals in 1958.
The player with the most goals in round of 16 is James Rodríguez, with 2 goals in 2014.
The player with the most goals in the group stage is Sándor Kocsis, with 11 goals in 1954.
The player with the most headed goals in history is Miroslav Klose, with 5 headed goals.
The player with the most penalty goals in history is Lothar Matthäus, with 4 penalty goals.
The player with the most free-kick goals in history is David Beckham, with 2 free-kick goals.
The player with the most volley goals in history is Diego Maradona, with 2 volley goals.
The player with the most bicycle-kick goals in history is Hugo Sánchez, with 1 bicycle-kick goal in 1986.
The player with the most heel-flick goals in history is Italian Filippo Inzaghi, with 1 heel-flick goal in 2006.
The player with the most rebound goals in history is Brazilian Ronaldo Nazário, with 3 rebound goals.
The player with the most counter-attack goals in history is German Miroslav Klose, with 4 counter-attack goals.
The player with the most corner-kick goals in history is English Bobby Charlton, with 2 corner-kick goals in 1966.
The player with the most missed penalties in history is Frenchman Michel Platini, with 2 misses.
The player with the most own goals in history is Mexican Manuel Rosas, with 1 own goal in 1930.
The player with the most hand goals in history is Argentine Diego Maradona, with 1 hand goal in 1986.
The player with the most headed goals in a single edition is German Miroslav Klose, with 5 headed goals in 2002.
The player with the most penalty goals in a single edition is English Harry Kane, with 3 penalty goals in 2018.
The player with the most free-kick goals in a single edition is Argentine Lionel Messi, with 2 free-kick goals in 2022.
The player with the most volley goals in a single edition is Italian Marco Tardelli, with 2 volley goals in 1982.
The player with the most bicycle-kick goals in a single edition is Mexican Hugo Sánchez, with 1 bicycle-kick goal in 1986.
The player with the most heel-flick goals in a single edition is Italian Filippo Inzaghi, with 1 heel-flick goal in 2006.
